ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for Indian spiced potato curry (aloo sabzi); closest defensible match is FOODON_03311737 (potato dish). Verified as a real FoodOn class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked potato preparations; used as closest defensible anchor for spiced Indian potato curry. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for aloo sabzi.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Indian potato curry dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 serving of potato curry / side dish
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of aloo curry is approximately 250 g, equivalent to a medium bowl served as a side dish alongside naan, roti, or rice.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Potato curry (aloo sabzi) reference values; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central (Potatoes, boiled, cooked in skin, flesh, without salt, SR Legacy #11356) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy 128 kcal, protein 2.4 g, fat 7.2 g, carbohydrate 14.0 g) are consistent with ICMR-NIN values for a lightly oiled spiced potato preparation. Micronutrients (potassium, vitamin B6, folate, magnesium, phosphorus) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for boiled potato flesh, adjusted downward to reflect dilution from added oil, tomato, and spices. Sodium 180 mg/100 g reflects added salt in restaurant-style preparation. All values are adapted estimates; no exact aloo sabzi entry exists in ICMR-NIN tabular data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Potato, boiled (GI ~65, glucose reference); Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indian aloo curry (spiced potato with oil and tomato). The closest approved analogous food is boiled potato (GI ~65, glucose reference) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. The addition of oil and spices may modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain boiled potato; however, GI 65 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 35.0) / 100 = 22.75, rounded to 22.8. Carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 14.0 × 2.5 = 35.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 180 mg/100 g; potassium ~320 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 180 mg/100 g (450 mg per 250 g serving) is below the high-concern threshold but meaningful for hypertensive individuals on a sodium-restricted diet; request reduced added salt and retain potato skin to maximise potassium intake, which supports the DASH dietary pattern.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~65) and glycemic load (~22.8 per serving); starchy potato base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Aloo curry has a medium-to-high GI (~65) and a high glycemic load (~22.8 per 250 g serving) driven by the starchy potato base; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 100–125 g, pair with high-fibre legumes or leafy greens, and consider allowing the potato to cool before reheating to increase resistant starch content and lower the effective glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.0 g/100 g; cholesterol 0 mg; plant-based dish
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aloo curry is a plant-based dish with low saturated fat (1.0 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol, making it broadly compatible with a heart-healthy diet; however, total fat at 7.2 g/100 g from cooking oil should be monitored in individuals with dyslipidaemia, and sodium intake should be managed to avoid compounding cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, green chilli) containing solanine
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Potatoes, tomatoes, and green chillies are all nightshade vegetables; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and confirmed nightshade sensitivity should avoid this dish or substitute potato with sweet potato and tomato with tamarind, while retaining anti-inflammatory spices such as turmeric and ginger.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo (आलू) is the Hindi word for potato and in the context of Indian cuisine refers to a spiced potato-based dish — most commonly aloo sabzi or aloo curry — in which diced or cubed potatoes are sautéed in oil with whole spices (cumin, mustard seeds) and then simmered in a tomato-onion gravy seasoned with turmeric, coriander, garam masala, green chilli, ginger, and garlic. The result is a soft, mildly spiced dish with a slightly tangy, earthy flavour and a thick, lightly oily gravy. In Singapore's Indian restaurant and hawker context, aloo is typically served as a side dish alongside naan, roti prata, or biryani rice. Nutritionally, aloo curry provides approximately 128 kcal per 100 g, with 14 g carbohydrate, 7.2 g fat (predominantly from cooking oil), and 2.4 g protein. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. Its glycemic index is estimated at ~65 (medium-high, analogous to boiled potato), yielding a high glycemic load of ~22.8 per 250 g serving, which is a significant consideration for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 180 mg/100 g is moderate and should be monitored by hypertensive individuals. The dish contains nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato, chilli) and is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and confirmed nightshade sensitivity. Potassium (~320 mg/100 g) and vitamin C (6 mg/100 g) contribute modest micronutrient value. Turmeric provides curcumin with anti-inflammatory properties.
common_user_queries: How many calories in aloo curry?
What is the GI of potato curry?
Can I eat aloo if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros are consistent with ICMR-NIN reference values for a lightly oiled spiced potato preparation and are internally coherent (energy check: 2.4×4 + 7.2×9 + 14.0×4 = 9.6 + 64.8 + 56.0 = 130.4 kcal, close to stated 128 kcal within rounding). No exact aloo sabzi tabular entry was found in ICMR-NIN; values are adapted estimates. Micronutrients are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central boiled potato data. GI is an analogous estimate from boiled potato (University of Sydney / Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): aloo curry is a fully plant-based dish containing no animal products; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in all plant foods. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): potatoes and plant-based spices contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero for this vegan preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no partially hydrogenated oils are used in traditional aloo sabzi preparation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.5 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for vegetable oil (assumed neutral cooking oil such as sunflower or canola) at the fat level of 7.2 g/100 g minus saturated fat 1.0 g; detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific preparation is not available in ICMR-NIN or Singapore HPB database as of 2026. 3. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on boiled potato (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for Indian aloo curry exists in approved sources. 4. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods) is an approved primary source for Indian dishes and is retained. The input data_source 'grabfood' is a forbidden source per Project Veritas v2 and has not been used as primary nutrient evidence. 5. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 (potato dish) and FoodEx2 A00LC (cooked potato preparations) are used as closest defensible approximate matches; no exact codes exist for Indian spiced potato curry. SNOMED CT omitted to preserve ontology honesty. 6. Singaporean dish context: this dish is served in Singapore's Indian restaurant and hawker context; full Singaporean multilingual naming (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) has been applied per G6 and Q1 rules.
